title: Runway and Taxiway Lighting System Status
spl:
index=airport sourcetype="airfield:lighting"
| eval fault=if(comm_ok=0 OR lower(alarm_state)!="normal" OR intensity_pct < 50, 1, 0)
| where fault=1
| stats latest(intensity_pct) as intensity, latest(alarm_state) as alarm, latest(comm_ok) as comm_ok by runway_id, circuit_id
| sort runway_id, circuit_id
| table runway_id, circuit_id, intensity, alarm, comm_ok
description: Lighting circuit faults affect night and low-visibility operations; consolidating SCADA alarms with last-known good states speeds electrical maintenance dispatch.
value: Service and risk leaders use signals from Runway and Taxiway Lighting System Status to move crews, budget, and customer or regulator communications while the situation is still controllable, so the organization steers from live data rather than a post-incident report.
implementation: Map SCADA points to runway/taxi identifiers used in NOTAM workflows. Do not replace airfield lighting control systems; Splunk is for visibility and trending. Filter planned test events via maintenance tags. **Domain context:** FAA/EASA lighting requirements tie to LVP/LVO operations—control remains in the field lighting system, not Splunk. **Splunk:** `comm_ok` and `intensity_pct` types must be numeric for comparisons.
visualization: Single value (circuits in fault), Table (runway × circuit), Timeline (alarm transitions).
knownFalsePositives: Lighting state noise while circuits are tagged out for re-lamp, airfield work, or AGL certification runs that the electrical maintenance package covers.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Airfield lighting SCADA via HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=airport` `sourcetype="airfield:lighting"` (circuit_id, runway_id, intensity_pct, comm_ok, alarm_state).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Map SCADA points to runway/taxi identifiers used in NOTAM workflows. Do not replace airfield lighting control systems; Splunk is for visibility and trending. Filter planned test events via maintenance tags. **Domain context:** FAA/EASA lighting requirements tie to LVP/LVO operations—control remains in the field lighting system, not Splunk. **Splunk:** `comm_ok` and `intensity_pct` types must be numeric for comparisons.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=airport sourcetype="airfield:lighting"
| eval fault=if(comm_ok=0 OR lower(alarm_state)!="normal" OR intensity_pct < 50, 1, 0)
| where fault=1
| stats latest(intensity_pct) as intensity, latest(alarm_state) as alarm, latest(comm_ok) as comm_ok by runway_id, circuit_id
| sort runway_id, circuit_id
| table runway_id, circuit_id, intensity, alarm, comm_ok
```

Understanding this SPL

**Runway and Taxiway Lighting System Status** — Lighting circuit faults affect night and low-visibility operations; consolidating SCADA alarms with last-known good states speeds electrical maintenance dispatch.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=airport` `sourcetype="airfield:lighting"` (circuit_id, runway_id, intensity_pct, comm_ok, alarm_state). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Airfield lighting SCADA via HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: airport; **sourcetype**: airfield:lighting. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=airport, sourcetype="airfield:lighting". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **fault** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where fault=1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by runway_id, circuit_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Pipeline stage (see **Runway and Taxiway Lighting System Status**): table runway_id, circuit_id, intensity, alarm, comm_ok


Step 3 — Validate
Compare alert rows with the airport operations or A-CDM tool and, where relevant, airline systems for the same flight or stand. Validate identifiers (flight, stand, belt) match your lookup tables.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (circuits in fault), Table (runway × circuit), Timeline (alarm transitions).
grandmaExplanation: We watch runway and taxi lighting circuits. We help you catch faults before night ops lose guidance.
